era_context: The early 14th century CE continues with the same major powers: the Delhi Sultanate, Yuan Dynasty, Ottoman Empire, and fragmented European kingdoms. The Mali Empire is at its height under Mansa Musa. This is the era immediately before the Black Death transforms Eurasia. Trade networks, particularly the Silk Road and Indian Ocean routes, are active and prosperous.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (limited written records from this period)

events.title: Mansa Musa's pilgrimage to Mecca (continued/return journey)
events.region: Mali Empire, West Africa; Egypt; Arabia
events.description: Mansa Musa's pilgrimage to Mecca, which began in 1323 or 1324, continued through 1325. Some sources place his return journey through Egypt in 1325. The pilgrimage is one of the most famous in Islamic history, notable for the enormous wealth displayed and the impact on Egyptian economy and Mali's international prestige. The exact chronology remains debated among scholars.
events.certainty_note: The pilgrimage spanned 1323–1325 or 1324–1325 depending on the source. The exact year of return is uncertain; 1325 is a plausible date for the return journey, but some sources suggest 1324.

events.title: Aztec founding of Tenochtitlan
events.description: According to Aztec tradition, the Mexica people founded the city of Tenochtitlan on an island in Lake Texcoco in 1325 CE. This date is recorded in Aztec codices and later Spanish colonial accounts. The founding followed a migration from Aztlán and was marked by the discovery of an eagle perched on a cactus, which the Mexica interpreted as a divine sign. Tenochtitlan would become one of the largest cities in the world by the 16th century. However, modern archaeological evidence suggests the site may have been occupied earlier, and the traditional date of 1325 may be symbolic or approximate.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (16th century, post-conquest Aztec codex)
Bernal Díaz del Castillo, The True History of the Conquest of New Spain (16th century)
Archaeological evidence from Mexico City excavations

events.certainty_note: The date 1325 comes from Aztec tradition recorded in codices and Spanish colonial sources. Modern archaeology suggests occupation of the site may have begun earlier (possibly 14th century, but not necessarily 1325). The traditional date is culturally significant but historically uncertain. Archaeological stratigraphy does not precisely confirm 1325.

events.title: Edward II's reign continues; political instability in England
events.description: Edward II of England continued his troubled reign in 1325, marked by conflicts with the nobility, military failures in Scotland, and personal scandals involving his favorites (particularly Piers Gaveston and later Hugh Despenser). The year 1325 saw ongoing baronial opposition to Edward II's rule. This instability would culminate in Isabella's invasion in 1326 and Edward II's deposition in 1327.
events.certainty_note: Edward II's reign and the political conflicts of 1325 are extensively documented in contemporary English records and chronicles.

events.title: Delhi Sultanate under Muhammad bin Tughluq
events.description: Muhammad bin Tughluq (r. 1325–1351) ascended to the throne of the Delhi Sultanate in 1325, succeeding his father Ghazi ud-Din Tughluq. Muhammad bin Tughluq is remembered as an ambitious but controversial ruler who attempted major administrative and monetary reforms. His reign saw significant territorial expansion but also economic disruption and rebellion. The early years of his reign (1325–1330) were marked by consolidation of power and ambitious projects.
key_figures: Muhammad bin Tughluq (Sultan of Delhi, r. 1325–1351)

sources.name: Ziauddin Barani, Tarikh-i Firoz Shahi (14th century, near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: Muhammad bin Tughluq's accession in 1325 is well-documented in contemporary Islamic chronicles and court records.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Tenochtitlan's founding (1325) is traditional and symbolic rather than archaeologically confirmed. Modern excavations have not definitively established 1325 as the founding date; the site may have been occupied earlier or the date may be approximate. The traditional Aztec date should be treated as legendary or approximate rather than confirmed historical fact. Regarding Mansa Musa's pilgrimage, the exact year of return (1325 vs. 1324) is disputed among sources.
historiographic_note: The record for 1325 is heavily weighted toward Islamic and European sources. English political history is well-documented through royal records and chronicles. Islamic sources (Ibn Battuta, Barani) provide detailed accounts of the Delhi Sultanate and Mali Empire, though with some chronological ambiguity. Mesoamerican history relies entirely on post-conquest Aztec codices and Spanish colonial accounts, making the dating of Tenochtitlan's founding uncertain. Sub-Saharan Africa, East Asia, and indigenous Americas are underrepresented in the surviving written record.

graph_edges.note: Muhammad bin Tughluq's reign lasted from 1325 to 1351
Political instability in England in 1325 led to Isabella's invasion in 1326
